title: Residual Blocks
concept: A residual block asks its learned layers to produce a useful adjustment to the input, then adds that adjustment back through a skip path instead of rebuilding the whole representation from scratch.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main job of a skip connection?

options.text: Carry a block's input forward along a shortcut so it can be combined later
Turn every convolution into max pooling

exercises.explain: Right — residual blocks build directly on that shortcut idea.

cards.title: Edit the photo, don't repaint the scene
cards.body: If a photo is already mostly good, you usually adjust contrast, sharpen an edge, or crop a distraction. You do not redraw the whole image from nothing. Residual blocks use the same mindset: keep the good base signal, learn the improvement.

cards.title: Input plus learned residual
cards.body: A residual block has a main learned path and a skip path. If the input is x, the learned layers produce a refinement often written as F(x), and the block outputs x + F(x). In plain language: copy the useful part forward, then add the change that helps.

cards.title: Renovation instead of demolition
cards.body: When a room already has good walls and lighting, a builder may add shelves, repaint, or swap the furniture layout. Starting from a full demolition would waste effort. Residual blocks make 'renovation' easy for a network.

cards.title: Clean up a wheel detector
cards.body: Suppose a feature map already captures the rough outline of a car wheel. The next block might only need to strengthen the circular rim and suppress a few background textures. A residual block lets the network keep the wheel evidence and learn that targeted cleanup as an added correction.

cards.title: Residual does not mean tiny forever
cards.body: A common myth is that residual blocks can only make tiny cosmetic changes. Not really. If the task needs a strong correction, the learned residual can be strong. The point is not to force smallness. The point is to make preserving a good base representation easy when that is useful.

cards.title: Rebuild everything vs. add a correction

left.text:
Must generate the whole next state.
Good old information can be overwritten.
Small fixes are harder to express cleanly.

right.text:
Shortcut carries input x.
Main path learns F(x).
Output becomes input plus refinement.

exercises.prompt: What is the main idea of a residual block?

options.text: Keep the input available and learn the adjustment that should be added to it
Throw away the input and predict labels inside the block
Replace every convolution with a file lookup table
Force all feature maps to stay identical forever

exercises.explain: Correct — residual blocks are built around 'input plus learned change.'

exercises.prompt: True or false: Residual blocks only work when the needed change is always tiny.
exercises.explain: False. Residual blocks often help with small refinements, but they can still learn large useful changes when needed.

exercises.sentence: A residual block usually outputs the input plus a learned ___ .
exercises.explain: Adjustment is right — the learned path supplies the change that improves the original signal.

exercises.prompt: Which situation sounds most like a residual block in action?

options.text: A block keeps a useful feature map and learns how to sharpen or clean it before adding the result back
A block deletes its input because preserving earlier features is never useful
A block changes the image label from cat to dog without using activations

exercises.explain: Exactly — residual design is about preserving a strong base and learning a correction on top.

exercises.prompt: A CNN already captures lane markings fairly well, but the next block should make those boundaries clearer and suppress road noise. Why is a residual block a natural fit?

options.text: Because the block can keep the lane evidence and learn the extra cleanup as a correction added on top
Because residual blocks work only when the input contains no useful information yet
Because the skip path prevents any new learned transformation from happening
Because residual blocks replace convolution with image compression

exercises.explain: Exactly. Residual blocks shine when the network should preserve a lot of what it already knows and add a targeted improvement. Next, we'll ask a bigger question: if depth can add power, why doesn't simply stacking more and more layers always work?
